metadata.title: The Happiness Paradox
learning_objectives: Read and understand an article about the science of happiness
Use 6 collocations related to well-being in context
Distinguish and use present perfect vs. simple past correctly
Evaluate ideas critically and support opinions with evidence
Speak about personal experiences using target grammar

content.text: Look at the photo. What is happening? How do the people look?

image.alt_text: Crowd of joyful people celebrating together
image.description: Vibrant crowd at a celebration, people laughing and smiling

content.text:
For generations, people have pursued happiness as if it were a destination — a place to arrive at once they secure the right job, earn enough money, or buy the perfect home. Yet research in positive psychology tells a surprisingly different story.

Psychologist Sonja Lyubomirsky has spent decades studying what makes people genuinely content. Her findings suggest that the circumstances of our lives — where we live, how much we earn, even our relationship status — account for only around ten percent of our long-term satisfaction. Genetics explains roughly fifty percent. The remaining forty percent comes down to our own thoughts and deliberate behaviours.

So what behaviours actually boost your mood and lead to lasting well-being? The evidence points strongly in one direction: other people. Study after study confirms that maintaining meaningful relationships is the single most important factor in human happiness. A landmark 75-year study by Harvard University followed hundreds of people from adolescence into old age and reached a striking conclusion: the quality of our relationships — not wealth, fame, or professional achievement — is the greatest predictor of a happy life.

Experiences also appear to matter more than possessions. People who spend money on doing things — travelling, attending concerts, learning new skills — consistently report higher levels of satisfaction than those who spend the same amount on material goods. One reason, researchers suggest, is that experiences become part of our identity and our personal story in a way that objects never can.

Perhaps most interesting of all is what happens when people pursue happiness as a direct goal. Paradoxically, those who treat happiness as something to be actively chased often end up feeling less of it. Happiness, it seems, is more of a by-product than a destination — something that tends to emerge when we live with a genuine sense of purpose: working towards something that matters, contributing to others, and remaining curious about the world around us.

The lesson from the science of well-being is clear: resist the pull of instant gratification. Invest instead in your connections, your experiences, and your curiosity — and happiness will quietly follow.
content.pull_quote: Happiness is not a destination. It is a by-product of a life well lived.

grammar_rule.structure: Present perfect: HAVE/HAS + past participle | Simple past: verb + -ed / irregular form
grammar_rule.use: Use the present perfect for past actions with present relevance or when no specific time is given. Use the simple past for finished actions at a specific past time.
notes: Time expressions like 'last year', 'in 2010', 'yesterday' signal simple past.
Time expressions like 'for', 'since', 'ever', 'never', 'already', 'just' often signal present perfect.
The present perfect connects the past to the present moment.

comparison_table.example_a: Scientists have studied happiness for decades.
comparison_table.use_a: Past action with present relevance; no specific time given
comparison_table.use_b: Finished action at a specific time in the past

comparison_table.example_a: He has never felt so content in his life.
comparison_table.example_b: They published their findings last year.

comparison_table.form_b: Refers to a finished, closed past
comparison_table.example_a: We have learned a lot about well-being recently.
comparison_table.example_b: The researchers completed their report in 2019.
comparison_table.use_a: The action or its effects are still relevant at the time of speaking
comparison_table.use_b: The action is over and belongs to a completed period of time

activities.instruction: Look at the photo and discuss with a partner.

items.prompt: What is happening in the photo? Describe what you see.
What makes people happy in your culture? Is it different from other cultures?

classroom.teacher_notes: Use the opening photo to activate schema. Take 3–4 responses. Note any interesting answers on the board. Don't pre-teach vocabulary.

activities.instruction: Read the article. Are these statements True (T) or False (F)?

items.question: According to Lyubomirsky, our life circumstances account for the majority of our happiness.

feedback.incorrect: Re-read paragraph 2. What percentage is given for circumstances?

feedback.correct: False. Circumstances account for only about 10%.

items.question: The Harvard study found that the quality of relationships predicts happiness more than wealth.

feedback.correct: Correct! The Harvard study concluded that the quality of relationships is the greatest predictor of a happy life.

feedback.incorrect: Re-read paragraph 3. What did the Harvard study conclude?

items.question: The article argues that spending money on possessions brings more satisfaction than experiences.

feedback.incorrect: Re-read paragraph 4. Does the article say possessions bring more satisfaction?

feedback.correct: False. The article argues that experiences bring more satisfaction than material possessions.

items.question: People who actively pursue happiness as a goal tend to feel happier.

feedback.incorrect: Re-read paragraph 5. What does the article say about chasing happiness directly?

feedback.correct: False. Paradoxically, actively chasing happiness often leads to feeling less of it.

classroom.teacher_notes: Students read silently (4 min). Then pairs compare answers before whole-class check.

activities.instruction: Answer these questions about the article.

items.question: What percentage of happiness comes from our own behaviours?
What does the Harvard study suggest is the most important factor in happiness?
According to the article, when does happiness tend to appear?
items.model_answer: When we live with a sense of purpose — working towards something, contributing to others, staying curious

activities.instruction: Find these phrases in the article. Match each to its meaning.

items.definition: to actively try to find or achieve happiness
items.example_sentence: Many people pursue happiness through their careers.

items.definition: to make yourself feel more positive or cheerful
items.example_sentence: Exercise is a great way to boost your mood.

items.definition: a lasting feeling that your life is going well
close connections with people who genuinely matter to you
items.example_sentence: Research shows meaningful relationships are key to happiness.

items.definition: pleasure or reward that comes immediately, without waiting
items.example_sentence: Social media often provides instant gratification.

items.definition: the feeling that your life and actions have real meaning
items.example_sentence: Volunteering gave her a strong sense of purpose.

classroom.teacher_notes: Have students find each phrase in the article first. Then reveal definitions. Drill pronunciation of longer phrases.

worksheet.print_instruction: Match each phrase to its definition.

activities.instruction: Complete the sentences with a phrase from Activity 4.

items.template: Regular exercise can {{0}} and reduce stress.

feedback.correct: Exactly right! 'Boost your mood' fits perfectly here.
feedback.incorrect: Think about which phrase means 'make you feel more positive'.

items.template: She found a real {{0}} in her work as a nurse.

feedback.correct: Well done! A nurse's work provides real meaning — a 'sense of purpose'.
feedback.incorrect: Think about which phrase means 'the feeling that your work has real meaning'.

items.template: Don't be tempted by {{0}} — save your money for experiences.

feedback.correct: Correct! 'Instant gratification' refers to the temptation of immediate rewards.
feedback.incorrect: Which phrase describes immediate rewards or pleasures that are hard to resist?

items.template: Studies show that {{0}} are the key to a happy life.

feedback.correct: Exactly! The article confirms that 'meaningful relationships' are central to happiness.
feedback.incorrect: Think about what the Harvard study identified as the key to happiness.

activities.instruction: Study the grammar table. Notice the difference.

items.sentence: Scientists have studied happiness for decades.
items.note: Present perfect: past action with present relevance. No specific time given.
Simple past: finished action at a specific time in the past.

items.sentence: He has never felt so content in his life.
items.note: Present perfect with 'never': life experience up to the present moment.

items.sentence: They published their findings last year.
items.note: Simple past: 'last year' signals a specific finished time.

classroom.teacher_notes: Cover the USE column. Ask students to identify the difference. Reveal. Ask: 'Which tense connects to NOW?' Draw a timeline on the board.

activities.instruction: Complete with the present perfect or simple past.

feedback.correct: Correct! We use the present perfect with 'for many years' — an unfinished time period.
feedback.incorrect: Is this a finished action at a specific time, or an ongoing action up to now? Use present perfect.

feedback.correct: Right! 'Last summer' tells us it's finished. Use simple past.
feedback.incorrect: The time marker 'last summer' signals a finished past action. Use simple past.

items.template: He {{0}} (never / feel) so happy in his life.

feedback.correct: Correct! 'Never' with a life experience uses the present perfect.
feedback.incorrect: Use 'has never felt' — the present perfect is used with 'never' for life experiences.

feedback.correct: Well done! 'In 2019' is a specific finished time, so use simple past.
feedback.incorrect: 'In 2019' tells us exactly when — a finished time in the past. Use simple past.

classroom.teacher_notes: Students complete individually first. Compare in pairs. Whole-class check — ask students to explain their choice.

activities.instruction: Rank these activities from 1 (most likely to bring lasting happiness) to 5 (least likely). Then compare with a partner and explain your choices.

items.rationale: Research suggests material possessions bring short-lived happiness. Link to instant gratification.

items.text: Spending an afternoon with close friends or family
items.rationale: Directly supports 'meaningful relationships' — the strongest predictor identified in the article.

items.text: Learning a new skill over several months
items.rationale: Linked to sense of purpose and long-term satisfaction.

items.text: Watching your favourite TV series all evening
items.rationale: Pleasant but passive — likely instant gratification with limited long-term benefit.

items.text: Doing something kind for a stranger, anonymously
items.rationale: Contributing to others is linked to purpose. Research supports this ('helper's high').

classroom.teacher_notes: Students rank individually (2 min). Then pair comparison (3 min). Take class vote — show of hands. Invite justification using article evidence.

worksheet.print_instruction: Number these activities 1–5 (1 = most likely to bring lasting happiness). Explain your top choice.

activities.instruction: Tell your partner about a time you felt genuinely happy.

items.prompt: Think about a time you felt truly happy.
sub_prompts: Where were you and who were you with?
How long did that feeling last?
useful_language: The last time I felt really happy was...
self_assessment_criteria: Did I use the present perfect or simple past correctly?
Did I listen to my partner's story?

classroom.teacher_notes: Model briefly with your own example. Pairs speak for 2–3 minutes each way. Ask 2–3 pairs to share with class.

items.prompt: What do you think is the most important factor for happiness? Use evidence from the article.
items.model_answer: I believe that meaningful relationships are the most important factor for happiness. According to the article, a 75-year Harvard study found that the quality of our relationships predicts happiness more than wealth or fame. In my own life, I have always felt happiest when I am with the people I care about.
